era_context: Early Iron Age II. Shalmaneser III is in the middle of his reign in Assyria. In the Levant, the traditional biblical chronology places this year within Solomon's reign (if dated c. 970–930 BCE) or shortly after. In Egypt, the 21st Dynasty is in its final years; the 22nd Dynasty (Libyan) will emerge within a few years. In China, the Western Zhou is in its later phase. The Mediterranean world is characterized by increasing trade and the consolidation of regional powers.

events.title: Shalmaneser III's military campaigns and tribute collection
events.description: Shalmaneser III continues his systematic military campaigns in the west. His annals record expeditions against various Levantine states, including Aramean kingdoms, Phoenician cities, and other powers. These campaigns are designed to establish Assyrian hegemony and collect tribute. The campaigns are documented in detail in his royal inscriptions and represent some of the most reliable historical documentation for the Iron Age Levant.
events.certainty_note: Shalmaneser III's campaigns are well-documented in cuneiform sources, but the specific year-by-year sequence depends on absolute chronology with ±2–3 year uncertainty.

events.title: Possible dating of Iron Age IIA settlement expansion in the Levant
events.description: During the Iron Age IIA period (c. 980–900 BCE), the Levant experiences significant settlement expansion and economic development. Trade networks are expanding, and regional polities are consolidating power. If Solomon's reign is dated to c. 970–930 BCE, then 965 BCE falls within this period of expansion and development. However, the scale and nature of this development are archaeologically contested.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Levantine sites (Tel Megiddo, Tel Hazor, Tel Gezer, Tel Dan, etc.)

events.certainty_note: Iron Age IIA chronology is based on pottery typology and occasional synchronisms with Near Eastern dated contexts. Absolute dating is uncertain by ±20–30 years.

events.title: Possible end of the 21st Dynasty in Egypt (traditional dating)
events.description: The 21st Dynasty (Tanite Period) of Egypt is in its final years. The dynasty, which ruled from c. 1070–945 BCE, is characterized by a decline in central authority and the rise of the priesthood of Amun at Thebes. The 22nd Dynasty (Libyan) will emerge within a few years, marking a shift in Egypt's political structure. The transition is marked by the rise of Libyan mercenaries and settlers who will eventually establish their own dynasty.
key_figures: Psusennes II (last pharaoh of the 21st Dynasty, traditional dating)

sources.name: Egyptian king lists and chronological sources (Abydos King List, Turin Papyrus, etc.)

events.certainty_note: Egyptian chronology for this period is based on king lists and astronomical calculations. The end of the 21st Dynasty is traditionally dated to c. 945 BCE, but there is uncertainty of ±5–10 years. The exact year of transition is not precisely known.
cross_references: Related to the emergence of the 22nd Dynasty (Libyan) c. 945 BCE

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the end of the 21st Dynasty is uncertain. Some sources place it at 945 BCE, others at 946 or 947 BCE. The transition to the 22nd Dynasty is not marked by a dramatic event but rather a gradual shift in power. The 21st Dynasty's decline is attributed to the rise of the priesthood of Amun and the increasing power of Libyan mercenaries, but the exact chronology is debated.
historiographic_note: For 965 BCE, the historical record is sparse. Mesopotamian sources (Shalmaneser III's annals) provide the most detailed documentation. Egyptian sources provide king lists and some administrative records, but contemporary documentation for this specific year is absent. The Levant is known from later biblical and Assyrian sources, but contemporary documentation for this specific year is absent. Most of the world has no written records for this year. The surviving sources are heavily biased toward elite political and military events in the Near East.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of Shalmaneser III's reign and military campaigns
The decline of the 21st Dynasty leads to the emergence of the 22nd Dynasty (Libyan) c. 945 BCE
